content.text: What are Identity Providers in External ID?

Microsoft Entra External ID (CIAM) allows customer-facing applications to accept sign-in from multiple external identity providers (IdPs). Rather than requiring customers to create yet another username and password, you federate with providers they already trust — Google, Facebook, Apple, or any OIDC/SAML-compatible system.

External ID acts as a federation hub: it presents a unified sign-in experience to the customer, handles IdP-specific protocol differences, normalizes claims into a consistent token, and then issues an OIDC/OAuth 2.0 token to your application. Your app only ever talks to one endpoint.
Supported Identity Provider Types
IdP Type
Protocol
Setup Complexity
Typical Use Case
Email One-Time Passcode
Built-in
None
Customers without a social account; secure fallback
Google
OAuth 2.0
Low — register app in Google Console
Consumer apps; large existing user base
Facebook
OAuth 2.0
Low — register app in Meta Developer portal
Consumer and gaming apps
Apple (Sign in with Apple)
OIDC
Medium — Apple Developer account required
iOS-first consumer apps
Microsoft Account (personal)
OIDC
None — available by default
Productivity apps used by consumers
Microsoft Entra ID (work)
OIDC
None — available by default
Apps used by employees of partner orgs
Custom OIDC
OIDC
Medium — discovery doc URL needed
Private or enterprise identity systems
Custom SAML
SAML 2.0
High — metadata XML exchange
Legacy enterprise or government IdPs
Email One-Time Passcode

Email OTP is the baseline identity provider that requires no external registration. When a customer provides an email address, External ID sends a six-digit code valid for 30 minutes. This covers customers who do not want to link a social account and is always available as a fallback.
Enabled by default — no API configuration needed
Code is delivered within seconds via the Microsoft notification pipeline
Code expires after 30 minutes; customers can request a new code
No user object is created in a separate directory — the email address becomes the user's local identifier
Supports sign-up, sign-in, and self-service password recovery flows within the same OTP mechanism
Configuring Google as a Social IdP
Google federation uses OAuth 2.0. You create a credential in the Google Cloud Console, then register the client ID and secret in the External ID tenant.
Step 1: Register an OAuth App in Google Cloud Console
Go to console.cloud.google.com → APIs & Services → Credentials.
Click Create Credentials → OAuth client ID → Web application.
Set Authorized redirect URI to: https://<tenant>.ciamlogin.com/te/<tenant>/oauth2/authresp
Copy the Client ID and Client Secret.
Step 2: Add Google IdP in External ID via Graph API

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'IdentityProvider.ReadWrite.All'

# Register Google as a social identity provider
$googleIdp = @{
    '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.socialIdentityProvider'
    displayName   = 'Google'
    identityProviderType = 'Google'
    clientId      = '<your-google-client-id>'
    clientSecret  = '<your-google-client-secret>'
}

New-MgIdentityProvider -BodyParameter $googleIdp
Write-Host 'Google IdP registered.'
Step 3: Add Google to the User Flow

# Link the Google IdP to a specific user flow
$userFlowId = 'B2C_1_SignUpSignIn'

# Get the Google IdP object ID
$googleIdpObj = Get-MgIdentityProvider | Where-Object { $_.DisplayName -eq 'Google' }

# Add to user flow identity providers
New-MgIdentityAuthenticationEventFlowAsExternalUsersSelfServiceSignUpIdentityProvider `
    -AuthenticationEventsFlowId $userFlowId `
    -BodyParameter @{ '@odata.id' = "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProviders/$($googleIdpObj.Id)" }

Write-Host 'Google IdP added to user flow.'
Configuring Facebook as a Social IdP
Facebook uses the Meta Developer platform. The redirect URI format is the same as Google. Only Facebook's own SDK tokens are accepted — third-party tokens are rejected.

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'IdentityProvider.ReadWrite.All'

# Register Facebook / Meta as a social identity provider
$fbIdp = @{
    '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.socialIdentityProvider'
    displayName   = 'Facebook'
    identityProviderType = 'Facebook'
    clientId      = '<meta-app-id>'
    clientSecret  = '<meta-app-secret>'
}

New-MgIdentityProvider -BodyParameter $fbIdp
Write-Host 'Facebook IdP registered.'
Configuring a Custom OIDC Identity Provider

Any OpenID Connect-compliant provider can be added as a custom IdP by supplying the metadata URL (discovery document). External ID fetches the metadata to discover the authorization and token endpoints, supported response types, and JWKS URI.

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'IdentityProvider.ReadWrite.All'

# Add a custom OIDC IdP (e.g. Okta, Auth0, PingIdentity)
$customOidc = @{
    '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.openIdConnectIdentityProvider'
    displayName   = 'Okta Corporate'
    clientId      = '<okta-client-id>'
    clientSecret  = '<okta-client-secret>'
    metadataUrl   = 'https://contoso.okta.com/.well-known/openid-configuration'
    domainHint    = 'contoso.com'
    # responseType can be 'code' or 'id_token'
    responseType  = 'code'
    # Scopes to request from the remote IdP
    scope         = 'openid profile email'
    # Map remote claim to local upn
    claimsMapping = @{
        userId      = 'sub'
        displayName = 'name'
        email       = 'email'
        givenName   = 'given_name'
        surname     = 'family_name'
    }
}

New-MgIdentityProvider -BodyParameter $customOidc
Write-Host 'Custom OIDC IdP registered.'
Domain Hints and Home Realm Discovery

When multiple IdPs are configured, External ID presents a picker to the customer. Domain hints skip the picker for users whose email domain is already associated with a specific IdP — reducing sign-in friction in B2B-adjacent CIAM scenarios.
Technique
How it works
Example
domain_hint parameter
App appends domain_hint=contoso.com to auth request; External ID routes directly to that IdP
Employees of a partner company using a dedicated portal
login_hint parameter
App supplies the user's email; External ID pre-fills and auto-selects IdP
Re-authentication after session timeout
IdP domainHint property
Set on the custom OIDC IdP object; automatically applied when email matches
Federated enterprise directory
Claims Mapping and Token Customization

Each IdP returns claims with different names and formats. External ID normalizes them into a standard set, but you can also define custom claims transformations via attributes collected during sign-up or via API connectors.
Viewing Registered IdPs and Their Claim Mappings
Custom Claims via Token Claims Mapping Policy
Auditing Identity Provider Activity
KQL: Sign-ins by Identity Provider

// Distribution of sign-ins across identity providers — last 30 days
SignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where AppDisplayName != ''
| extend IdPType = tostring(AuthenticationDetails[0].authenticationMethod)
| extend FederatedIdP = coalesce(tostring(parse_json(tostring(AuthenticationProcessingDetails))[0].value), 'Local')
| summarize SignInCount = count() by FederatedIdP, ResultType
| where ResultType == 0
| order by SignInCount desc
KQL: IdP Registration and Deletion Events

AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(90d)
| where OperationName has_any ('Add identity provider', 'Delete identity provider', 'Update identity provider')
| project
    TimeGenerated,
    OperationName,
    Actor = InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName,
    TargetIdP = tostring(TargetResources[0].displayName),
    Result
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Common Issues and Troubleshooting
Symptom
Likely Cause
Fix
Google button missing from sign-in page
Google IdP not linked to the user flow
Add IdP to the user flow identity providers list
Error AADSTS70011 from Google federation
Redirect URI mismatch between Google Console and tenant
Verify redirect URI is exactly https://<tenant>.ciamlogin.com/te/<tenant>/oauth2/authresp
Custom OIDC IdP fails metadata fetch
Metadata URL returns non-200 or malformed OIDC discovery document
Test the URL in browser; ensure it returns a valid JSON discovery document
Claims not appearing in token
Claim not included in claimsMapping or not requested in scope
Add the claim name to scope and claimsMapping.email/givenName/surname
Users land on wrong IdP automatically
Stale domainHint or login_hint in cached app link
Flush app-side cached auth parameters; test with a fresh incognito session
Configuring Apple Sign In
Apple Sign In uses OIDC with a private key JWT client assertion instead of a client secret. You must have an Apple Developer account, create a Services ID, and generate a private key.
Prerequisites in Apple Developer Portal
Enroll in the Apple Developer Program (paid account required).
Create a Services ID (the OAuth client_id) with Sign In with Apple enabled.
Register the authorized redirect domain: <tenant>.ciamlogin.com
Create a private key with Sign In with Apple capability; download the .p8 file.
Note the Key ID and Team ID from the developer portal.
Register Apple IdP via Graph API

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'IdentityProvider.ReadWrite.All'

# Apple uses a JWT client secret generated from the .p8 private key.
# The secret is valid for up to 6 months; rotate before expiry.
# Generate the JWT using your preferred JWT library or the Apple documentation.
$appleJwt = '<your-generated-apple-client-secret-jwt>'

$appleIdp = @{
    '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.appleManagedIdentityProvider'
    displayName   = 'Apple'
    # The Services ID from Apple Developer portal
    developerId   = '<10-char-team-id>'
    serviceId     = 'com.contoso.app'
    keyId         = '<10-char-key-id>'
    certificateData = Get-Content './AuthKey_<KeyID>.p8' -Raw
}

New-MgIdentityProvider -BodyParameter $appleIdp
Write-Host 'Apple IdP registered. Remember to rotate the private key before 6 months.'
Removing an Identity Provider

When an IdP is no longer needed, remove it from all user flows first before deleting the IdP object. Deleting an IdP that is still linked to a user flow causes sign-in failures for users who used that provider.
Terraform: Managing Social IdPs as Code
The AzureAD Terraform provider exposes the azuread_identity_provider resource for managing social and custom OIDC providers declaratively.
Security Considerations for Social IdPs

Federating with social IdPs shifts authentication trust to the external provider. Understand what each provider guarantees — and does not guarantee — about user identity before granting access to sensitive resources.
Consideration
Details
Mitigation
Account takeover at the IdP
If a customer's Google account is compromised, their External ID account is also compromised
Require MFA at the External ID layer via Conditional Access even for social sign-ins
Email address changes at the IdP
Google allows users to change their primary email; External ID maps on email by default
Use the IdP-issued subject claim (sub) as the stable identifier, not email
Token replay attacks
ID tokens from social IdPs have a validity window during which replays are possible
External ID validates nonce, iat, and exp; no app-side action needed
Scope creep
Requesting too many scopes from the social IdP increases the blast radius of a token leak
Request only openid, profile, and email; avoid requesting social-API-specific scopes
IdP secret rotation
OAuth client secrets and Apple JWT keys expire; rotation failure breaks sign-in
Store secrets in Azure Key Vault; set calendar alerts 30 days before expiry
Summary
External ID supports Email OTP (built-in), Google, Facebook, Apple, Microsoft Account, Microsoft Entra ID, custom OIDC, and custom SAML identity providers.
Social IdPs (Google, Facebook) require registering an OAuth app in the provider's developer console, then adding the client ID/secret to the External ID tenant.
Custom OIDC providers are added via a metadata URL (discovery document) and a claimsMapping that maps remote claims to the standard External ID schema.
Domain hints allow applications to bypass the IdP picker for users with known email domains, reducing sign-in friction.
Audit logs and SignInLogs in Log Analytics provide visibility into per-IdP sign-in distribution and configuration change history.
All IdP operations are available through the Microsoft Graph API under the /identityProviders resource, enabling Terraform and PowerShell automation.
